parentQuestion: The three basic regions of the cerebrum are the cerebral cortical gray matter, internal white matter, and the superior and inferior colliculi.

options: The largest part of the brain (about 83% of brain mass)
scaffolds.explain: The CEREBRUM is the LARGEST PART of the brain, making up about 83% of brain mass. It's the big wrinkled dome you picture when you think 'brain.'

wrongExplains.0: The spinal cord is below the brain. The cerebrum is the large upper portion of the brain.
wrongExplains.2: The cerebrum is brain tissue, not bone. The skull bones protect the cerebrum.
wrongExplains.3: The cerebrum is neural tissue. Muscles are in the body for movement.

options: Neuron cell bodies where processing happens
scaffolds.explain: GRAY MATTER contains NEURON CELL BODIES, dendrites, and synapses — the computational hardware. This is where the actual thinking and processing happens.

wrongExplains.0: Myelinated axon bundles are white matter. Gray matter has cell bodies.
wrongExplains.2: Gray matter is neural tissue, not bone.
wrongExplains.3: While gray matter has blood supply, it's defined by neuron cell bodies, not blood vessels.

options: Myelinated axons that transmit signals between brain regions
scaffolds.explain: WHITE MATTER contains MYELINATED AXONS that carry signals between brain regions. The fatty myelin coating makes it appear white — it's the brain's wiring system.

wrongExplains.0: Neuron cell bodies are in gray matter. White matter has axons, not cell bodies.
wrongExplains.2: The skull is bone. White matter is neural tissue inside the brain.
wrongExplains.3: CSF is fluid in the ventricles. White matter is neural tissue made of axon bundles.

options: The thin outer layer of gray matter covering the cerebrum
scaffolds.explain: The CEREBRAL CORTEX is the THIN OUTER LAYER (2-4mm) of GRAY MATTER covering the cerebrum's surface. This is where conscious thought and processing occur.

wrongExplains.0: The cortex is the outer layer, not deep tissue. Deep structures include basal nuclei.
wrongExplains.2: The cortex is neural tissue, not bone.
wrongExplains.3: The cortex is part of the brain. The spinal cord is below the brain.

options: Deep clusters of gray matter inside the cerebrum
scaffolds.explain: The BASAL NUCLEI (also called basal ganglia) are DEEP CLUSTERS of GRAY MATTER inside the cerebrum. They help filter and coordinate movement.

wrongExplains.0: The outer surface is the cortex. The basal nuclei are deep internal structures.
wrongExplains.2: The basal nuclei are neural tissue, not bones.
wrongExplains.3: The basal nuclei are in the cerebrum, not the spinal cord.

scaffolds.q: Where are the superior and inferior colliculi located?
options: On the back of the midbrain (brainstem)
scaffolds.explain: The COLLICULI are on the back of the MIDBRAIN (part of the brainstem). They handle visual and auditory reflexes. They are NOT cerebrum structures.

wrongExplains.0: The colliculi are NOT in the cerebrum — they're in the midbrain below it.
wrongExplains.2: The colliculi are in the midbrain, not the spinal cord.
wrongExplains.3: The colliculi are neural structures, not skull bones.

scaffolds.q: What are the three TRUE regions of the cerebrum?
options: Cortex (gray matter), internal white matter, and basal nuclei
Superior colliculus, inferior colliculus, and midbrain
scaffolds.explain: The cerebrum has THREE REGIONS: (1) CORTICAL GRAY MATTER (outer processing layer), (2) INTERNAL WHITE MATTER (transmission highways), (3) BASAL NUCLEI (deep gray matter islands).

wrongExplains.0: Those are three major brain divisions, but not the three regions of the cerebrum specifically.
wrongExplains.2: The colliculi and midbrain are brainstem structures, not cerebrum regions.
wrongExplains.3: These are different structural categories entirely, not cerebrum regions.

scaffolds.q: Why is the parent statement FALSE?
options: The colliculi are midbrain structures, not cerebrum regions
White matter isn't inside the cerebrum
scaffolds.explain: The statement is FALSE because the COLLICULI ARE MIDBRAIN STRUCTURES, not cerebrum regions. The cerebrum's three regions are cortex, white matter, and basal nuclei.

wrongExplains.0: The cerebrum definitely has regions — cortex, white matter, and basal nuclei.
wrongExplains.2: Gray matter definitely exists — it contains neuron cell bodies.
wrongExplains.3: White matter is definitely inside the cerebrum — it forms the internal transmission system.

scaffolds.q: Which structure does NOT belong in the cerebrum?
scaffolds.explain: The COLLICULI do NOT belong in the cerebrum — they're MIDBRAIN (brainstem) structures. The statement tries to sneak midbrain structures into the cerebrum, making it FALSE.

wrongExplains.0: The cerebral cortex is the outer gray matter layer of the cerebrum — it definitely belongs.
wrongExplains.1: Internal white matter is the transmission system inside the cerebrum — it definitely belongs.
wrongExplains.3: The basal nuclei are deep gray matter inside the cerebrum — they definitely belong.
